video_title: Bitcoin's Next Move Depends On One Fed Decision
gist: Jordi Visser tells Anthony Pompliano the AI-stock selloff (Micron, Marvell, Caterpillar, etc.) is a leverage-driven 'midcycle slowdown,' not a bubble bursting, and lays out where he's still buying (memory, Vera Rubin-linked names, Bitcoin, Ethereum), why he isn't worried about AI capex debt or Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's inflation-data skepticism, and why 'multiple compression' rather than a dot-com-style crash is the real multi-year risk to public equities.

insights.claim: Jordi Visser argues the simultaneous selloff across high-flying AI names (Micron, Marvell, Caterpillar) while the equal-weight S&P and market breadth hit new highs reflects a 'second derivative' earnings deceleration — the same pattern Nvidia went through in 2024 — not a fundamental break in the AI trade.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Investors should read the AI-stock pullback as a slowing rate of earnings acceleration, not a broken thesis — a reason to sit through volatility in already-3x-to-10x-return names rather than exit the trade.
insights.quote: the AI midcycle slowdown is the second derivative change

insights.claim: Nvidia rose roughly 12x from ChatGPT's launch to June 2024 but only about 48% further over the next two years despite continued earnings growth — Visser uses this as the template for what other 3x-to-10x AI winners like Micron should now expect: strong but far slower forward returns.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Sets a realistic forward-return bar (20-40%/year, not another multi-bagger) for investors still holding post-parabolic AI infrastructure names.

insights.claim: A broad-based deleveraging — record-fast drawdowns in gold and silver, a collapsing technology momentum factor, closed Korean margin accounts, and large prime-broker-reported losses at multi-strat and quant funds — is forcing volatility-targeting strategies to cut position sizes, which Visser says explains the correlated 30-50% pullbacks in names like Micron.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: The AI-stock decline is a mechanical, leverage-driven event (vol-targeting and margin calls), not new fundamental information — a distinction that matters for whether the pullback is a buying opportunity or a trend change.

insights.claim: Visser expects names that broke out of multi-year trading ranges (e.g., Micron was flat from 2017 through the 'Liberation Day' lows) and are far above their 200-day moving average to hold within a 30-60% retracement and resume higher, while calling the 'fireworks show' phase of the rally over.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete drawdown band (30-60% from highs) for gauging when former multi-year range-breakout AI names are likely to bottom versus keep falling.

themes.theme: Picking Winners Within AI: Memory & The Earnings-Beat Framework

insights.claim: Visser's stock-picking rule is to hold names that haven't yet hit their earnings 'step-up' inflection (the point, as with Nvidia post-2024, where beats shrink from massive to marginal) — right now that means memory (Micron, Samsung, ASML all beat this quarter) and select names tied to Nvidia's Vera Rubin platform, on which he is publishing a dedicated paid research piece.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A concrete screen for rotating within the AI trade: prioritize names still beating earnings by a wide margin over those (like Nvidia now) beating by only a little.
insights.quote: the names that I'm focused on are the ones that I still think are going to beat earnings by a significant amount in the quarters to come

insights.claim: Visser runs a 100-name portfolio organized around equal-weight thematic baskets — an AI-thematic basket and a separate 'crypto 40' basket of six public companies (including PayPal and Robinhood) positioned for the crossover between AI agents and crypto/payments.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A concrete public-equity watchlist (PayPal, Robinhood, plus four other unnamed names) for investors tracking the agentic-payments crossover theme.

themes.theme: Open Source vs. Closed Models — Why Enterprises Won't Switch

insights.claim: Even though a new Chinese open-source model release ('Kimmy K3') matches or beats American frontier closed models on benchmarks, Visser doesn't expect U.S. Fortune 500 enterprises to adopt it because of hardware-access barriers, slow corporate decision cycles, and unresolved concerns about the cultural values embedded in the model's training.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the narrative that a competitive Chinese open-source release threatens American model providers' enterprise revenue in the near term.
insights.quote: I don't see the Fortune 500 companies in the United States switching to a Chinese model anytime soon.

insights.claim: Visser flags an under-discussed risk he calls 'cultural weights' — it's unclear whether a model's training data embeds the political-economic values of where it was built (e.g. more capitalist- vs. socialist-leaning responses), which could make customer-facing enterprise deployments of Chinese open-source models uncomfortable for Western companies.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A novel enterprise-adoption risk factor (beyond cost/performance) to watch for when evaluating open-source Chinese model uptake outside China.

insights.claim: Even self-hosting an open-source model locally doesn't guarantee data safety, because any instance that ever touches the internet is exposed to undisclosed backdoors that could be baked into the model weights — a risk Visser says pushes serious enterprise use back toward U.S. frontier providers and their cloud/cyber ecosystems (Google, Amazon, Microsoft).
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A concrete bear case against 'self-hosted open source is automatically safer' as an enterprise argument.

insights.claim: Anthony Pompliano says his product Sylvia already runs a live model router that sends simple queries to cheaper, lower-intelligence models and complex ones to the most powerful model, and reports the router is already having a measurable positive cost impact.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: A working example of the 'model router' architecture enterprises are expected to adopt broadly — evidence it's already delivering cost savings in production, not just theoretical.

themes.theme: Agentic AI Is Still Early — TAM & Model Economics

insights.claim: Visser, citing Brad Gerstner's recent All-In Pod appearance, frames AI as monetizing intelligence itself and therefore as 'the largest TAM that will ever exist' on Earth — meaning multiple model providers (he estimates roughly six) can all be profitable rather than the market being winner-take-all.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Argues against a zero-sum framing of the AI model-provider race — investors shouldn't need to pick a single winner to profit from the buildout.
insights.quote: this is the largest TAM that will ever exist, at least on planet Earth.

insights.claim: Visser is skeptical of forecasts that Anthropic's ARR jumps roughly 10x again next year to about a trillion dollars; he'd only underwrite roughly a doubling to about $200 billion, arguing that competition will compress growth the way it already has for Nvidia.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A specific, falsifiable ceiling (~$200B ARR, not ~$1T) to check Anthropic's growth trajectory against over the next year.

insights.claim: Visser says the market has 'barely entered the agentic world' — consumer agents haven't launched yet and even enterprise agents are barely being used — implying the compute/token demand curve implied by agentic AI is still in its earliest innings.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Supports staying long AI infrastructure/compute exposure on the view that today's usage understates the eventual demand base once consumer agents launch.
insights.quote: We have barely entered the agentic world. Barely.

insights.claim: Visser expects every company — including 'physical' businesses like energy and memory-chip makers — to face rising AI/AGI disruption risk within roughly three years, citing DeepMind's Demis Hassabis's public statement that the world is going to change over the next three years, which Visser treats as credible given Hassabis has publicly forecast AGI timing (around 2030) since 2010.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Sets an approximate 3-year window for when AGI-driven disruption risk becomes a live consideration for terminal-value analysis on public companies broadly, not just software.
insights.quote: the world is going to change over the next three years

insights.claim: Visser's central crypto thesis is that Bitcoin is the one major asset class immune to AI-driven disruption, which is why he holds it as a core position even while running an otherwise AI-thematic portfolio.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Frames Bitcoin's investment case less around debasement/scarcity and more as the one asset with no earnings stream for AI to commoditize away.
insights.quote: Bitcoin is the only thing that has a moat

insights.claim: On a volatility-adjusted basis, Bitcoin is far cheaper to own than AI-thematic exposure right now: Bitcoin's vol sits around 30 versus roughly 100 for the AI factor, meaning a portfolio can hold about three times as much Bitcoin as AI exposure for the same risk budget.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A concrete vol-adjusted sizing rule (~3:1 Bitcoin-to-AI) for investors rebalancing between the two themes after the AI-stock deleveraging.
insights.quote: Bitcoin V is still at 30. So if you're putting an asset in a portfolio right now, you can have three times as much uh Bitcoin on a V adjusted basis as you can AI.

insights.claim: Visser's entire crypto allocation is concentrated in just three names: Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Micro Strategy, with no other individual token or crypto-equity picks.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A simple, replicable crypto-allocation template from a macro investor who explicitly avoids picking individual altcoins.

insights.claim: Ethereum is up roughly 20% month-to-date as of the prior close, which would make it the best month since the prior August and puts it outperforming Bitcoin by the widest margin since then — a move Visser links to Ethereum's exposure to tokenization, agentic payments, and real-world-asset revenue rather than a pure store-of-value case.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Flags Ethereum's relative-strength breakout versus Bitcoin as a signal of rotation toward crypto's productive/revenue-bearing assets, not just the store-of-value trade.
insights.quote: it was up close to 20% monthto date

insights.claim: Visser cites the Stripe bid for PayPal as a validating signal for the AI-agent-meets-crypto/payments trade; PayPal sits inside his 'crypto 40' basket of six public companies positioned for that crossover.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A concrete M&A data point supporting the thesis that legacy payments companies are becoming AI-agent/crypto infrastructure plays.

insights.claim: Visser is trading Ethereum tactically until it clears its 200-day moving average, which he calls the technical signal for a broader new leg higher and wider rotation of capital into ETH.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Gives traders a specific technical trigger (200-day MA reclaim) to watch for confirmation of Visser's Ethereum-rotation thesis.

insights.claim: Visser reads the latest inflation report as broadly disinflationary outside one outlier metric (PCE core) — median inflation, trimmed-mean, core CPI, and sticky-price inflation all fell — leading him to call inflation 'a non-story for the rest of the year' barring a fluke in core services.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Supports positioning for continued Fed rate-cut/easing expectations rather than a hawkish surprise through year-end.
insights.quote: the inflation side is a non-story for the rest of the year.

insights.claim: Visser notes markets have priced the odds of a July rate hike down to just 10%, and he doesn't expect Fed Chair Kevin Warsh to hike in September or October either since that would land too close to the midterm elections — meaning July is the last realistic hike window this cycle.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A specific, checkable calendar prediction (no hike after July) for traders positioning around FOMC meetings this year.

insights.claim: Visser frames Kevin Warsh's approach as institutional 'reform,' not a hawkish or dovish stance — Warsh believes official inflation data is structurally unreliable (backward-looking, heavily revised) and, with a capital-markets background like Treasury Secretary Bessent, is well suited to lead the Fed at a moment when AI and digital assets both require financially literate policymakers.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Reframes Warsh's Fed leadership as structurally bullish for digital assets and market-sensitive policy, independent of the near-term rate path.

insights.claim: Visser argues persistent public anger over living costs is less about the current inflation rate (3.5% vs. 3.9%) and more a 'psychological' anchoring effect — consumers still mentally price goods against pre-COVID levels and the 2021-22 price shock, compounded by AI-driven job insecurity and political polarization giving people someone to blame.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Explains why consumer sentiment can stay depressed even as headline inflation cools — a gap that could persist for years and matters for reading consumer-spending and midterm-election-driven policy signals.

themes.theme: No Fear on the Debt — Deficit Spending Is Bullish for Crypto & AI Capex

insights.claim: Visser dismisses concerns that surging AI capex is dangerously debt-funded, noting hyperscaler balance sheets carry minimal leverage and that Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and Oracle together hold roughly $2 trillion of contracted RPO (remaining performance obligations) — already-secured demand he says funds the buildout even before new borrowing.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Directly counters the 'AI capex debt bubble' bear case with a specific balance-sheet figure ($2T contracted RPO across the four largest hyperscalers).

insights.claim: Visser's structural bull case for crypto rests on the persistent 5-6% U.S. budget deficit, which he calls ongoing debasement that's 'good for nominal GDP'; he expects Washington to keep funding the AI buildout aggressively regardless of cost because losing the AI race to China is politically unacceptable.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Ties the crypto debasement trade directly to an ongoing US-China AI competition narrative, giving it a durable macro catalyst beyond monetary policy alone.

themes.theme: Multiple Compression, Not a Dot-Com Bubble

insights.claim: Visser rejects the dot-com bubble comparison on demographic grounds: today's wealth is concentrated in largely passive, advisor-managed baby-boomer portfolios rather than the direct, high-risk retail speculation that defined 1999-2000.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the most common AI-bubble analogy, arguing the retail-leverage dynamics that drove the dot-com crash aren't present this cycle.

insights.claim: Visser's real long-term bear case isn't a bubble pop but 'multiple compression' — as AGI nears, public-company earnings could stay strong even as market multiples shrink, because commoditized AI tokens make it easier for lean AI-native private competitors to underprice incumbents (a pressure he says is already visible on Adobe and Salesforce).
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Reframes the multi-year AI risk for public equities from 'crash' to 'the S&P growing slower than earnings' — a distinct, already-observable pattern investors should track via the market's P/E versus EPS growth spread.
insights.quote: I actually believe that multiple compression story will become a much bigger story.

insights.claim: Visser draws an oil/fracking analogy: unlike fracking, where a technology shock overwhelmed linear demand growth and permanently capped oil's re-rating, AI infrastructure hasn't yet solved its supply-side bottleneck — years of flat capex (Micron's stock was unchanged from 2017 to the 'Liberation Day' lows) left the industry without built capacity, implying more room for AI infrastructure names to re-rate than energy ever had.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Offers a specific counter-analogy to the bearish 'AI capex will crash pricing like fracking did to oil' argument, based on capacity build-out timing rather than commodity economics.

themes.theme: Physical AI / Robotics as the Next Leg

insights.claim: Visser sees embodied AI (robotics) as the next major capital rotation within the AI trade, but says he's currently playing it as a semiconductor/compute trade rather than buying robotics-pure-play stocks directly, citing Elon Musk building large compute clusters for Tesla's robotics ambitions because of chip scarcity.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Suggests investors seeking robotics exposure today get better risk-adjusted access through the semiconductor supply chain than through early-stage robotics equities.
